answers.intake_q1: Can you describe the chest pain? For example, is it sharp, dull, pressure-like, or burning?
answers.intake_q2: When did the chest pain start?
answers.intake_q3: Where exactly is the chest pain located, and does it radiate anywhere?
answers.intake_a3: next to my sternum and it doesn't radiate
answers.intake_q4: Any shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or palpitations with the chest pain?
answers.intake_q5: Any recent trauma, heavy lifting, or coughing that might have triggered the chest pain?
answers.intake_q6: Any fever, chills, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q7: Any pain with deep breaths or when you press on the chest area?
answers.intake_q8: Any history of heart disease, lung problems, or blood clots?
answers.intake_q9: Any swelling, redness, or warmth over the painful area on your chest?
answers.intake_q10: Have you noticed any recent unexplained weight loss, night sweats, or fatigue?

gating_notes: liver disease status is yes/unknown (avoid hepatotoxic meds / adjust dosing)

note.soap: S: The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with sharp chest pain located next to the sternum that started one week ago. The pain does not radiate and is associated with tenderness on palpation and worsens with deep breaths. There are no accompanying symptoms such as shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, palpitations, fever, chills, or recent infections. The pain began after heavy lifting. She denies any history of heart disease, lung problems, or blood clots. There is no swelling, redness, or warmth over the painful area, and no recent unexplained weight loss, night sweats, or fatigue. She has no known medication allergies, is not pregnant, and has known liver disease but no renal disease. O: Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available. A: The working diagnosis is costochondritis, an inflammation of the costal cartilage causing localized chest pain that is reproducible with palpation and worsened by movement or deep breathing. This is consistent with the history of heavy lifting and absence of systemic symptoms or cardiopulmonary signs. Differential: 1. Costochondritis 2. Musculoskeletal chest wall strain or injury 3. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) 4. Less likely cardiac causes given absence of radiation, exertional symptoms, or associated autonomic features P: - First-line medication: - Acetaminophen, dosed per label instructions for 7–10 days, avoiding overdose and considering liver disease status - Alternatives: - Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), such as ibuprofen, dosed per label instructions for 7–10 days, excluding patients with liver disease complications . Given the patient’s liver disease, NSAIDs should be used cautiously or avoided if liver function is significantly impaired. (avoid in renal disease; especially severe CKD/dialysis) - Physical therapy and activity modification to avoid heavy lifting or movements that exacerbate pain - Local heat application for symptomatic relief - Medication safety considerations: The patient has liver disease, which increases the risk of hepatotoxicity with acetaminophen and potential adverse effects with NSAIDs. Avoid or use these medications with caution and monitor liver function if treatment is initiated. - Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek urgent care if chest pain worsens, becomes constant, radiates, or is associated with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, palpitations, or syncope. - Testing: If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).

evidence.title: Congenital heart disease - NHS
evidence.snippet: do not appear until you're a teenager or adult. Common symptoms of congenital heart disease include: feeling tired all the time shortness of breath being sweatier than usual, but skin feels cool and clammy heart palpitations - when your heartbeat feels uncomfortable or unusual areas of the skin and lips turning blue or grey ( cyanosis ) - this may be easier…

evidence.title: content.txt
evidence.snippet: Costochondritis is inflammation where your ribs join the bone in the middle of your chest (breastbone). It can cause sharp chest pain, especially when moving or breathing. It usually gets better on its own over time. Symptoms of costochondritis Costochondritis may cause sharp pain in the front or side of your chest. The pain may be worse when: - moving your…
